era_context: The early 14th century: the Yuan Dynasty in China is facing increasing instability; the Genkō Rebellion in Japan is ongoing (1331–1333); the Hundred Years' War between England and France is in its early diplomatic phase; the Ottoman Empire is consolidating power in Anatolia; the Avignon Papacy continues; the Mali Empire is in decline in West Africa.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Mesoamerica and South America (post-conquest sources only)
Central Asia and the Steppe (limited written records)
Southeast Asia (limited written records in European sources)

events.title: Genkō Rebellion continues in Japan
events.description: The Genkō Rebellion (1331–1333) continued in 1332 with ongoing military campaigns by Emperor Go-Daigo's forces and their samurai allies against the Hōjō Regency. The year 1332 saw significant battles and the consolidation of anti-Hōjō forces. The rebellion would culminate in 1333 with the fall of Kamakura and the destruction of Hōjō power. The 1332 campaigns were crucial in building momentum toward the final victory.

sources.name: Genkō Heiki (Records of the Genkō Rebellion)

events.certainty_note: The Genkō Rebellion is well-documented in Japanese chronicles. The year 1332 is part of the confirmed rebellion period (1331–1333).

events.title: Famine in Scotland
events.description: Scotland experienced a severe famine in 1332, likely related to climatic stress and poor harvests. The famine coincided with military campaigns during the Second War of Scottish Independence (1332–1357), when Edward Balliol and English forces invaded Scotland. The combination of warfare and famine created severe hardship for the Scottish population. Contemporary Scottish chronicles record the famine, though details are sparse.
key_figures: Edward Balliol (English-backed claimant to Scottish throne)
David II of Scotland (legitimate king, in exile)
events.certainty_note: Scottish sources record a famine in the early 1330s; 1332 is the traditional date, but the exact year is uncertain. The famine is attested in later compilations but not in contemporary primary sources.
cross_references: Contemporary with 1332-003 (Second War of Scottish Independence)

events.title: Second War of Scottish Independence begins
events.description: The Second War of Scottish Independence (1332–1357) began in 1332 when Edward Balliol, a claimant to the Scottish throne backed by Edward III of England, invaded Scotland. Balliol defeated the Scottish forces at the Battle of Dupplin Moor in August 1332 and briefly claimed the throne. However, Scottish resistance, led by the legitimate king David II (in exile) and his supporters, continued throughout the 1330s–1350s. The war was part of the broader Anglo-Scottish conflicts that characterized the medieval period.
events.certainty_note: The Second War of Scottish Independence and the Battle of Dupplin Moor in 1332 are well-documented in English and Scottish sources. The date is secure.
cross_references: Related to 1333-004 (Edward III's claims to France)

events.title: Yuan Dynasty faces peasant rebellions
events.description: The Yuan Dynasty in 1332 faced increasing peasant unrest and localized rebellions, part of a broader pattern of instability that would culminate in the collapse of Yuan rule by 1368. The rebellions were often motivated by economic hardship, Mongol rule, and religious movements (including Manichaean and Buddhist sects). While no single rebellion in 1332 was decisive, the cumulative effect of these uprisings weakened Yuan authority and contributed to the dynasty's eventual fall. The Red Turban Rebellion, which would become the major anti-Yuan movement, was beginning to organize in this period.

sources.name: Yuan Shi (History of the Yuan Dynasty), compiled 14th century

events.certainty_note: Chinese sources record peasant rebellions in the early 1330s, but specific dating to 1332 is uncertain. The rebellions are attested in later compilations (Yuan Shi, Ming Shilu) but not in contemporary primary sources.
cross_references: Led to 1334-002 (Zhu Yuanzhang joins Red Turban Rebellion)

disconfirming_evidence: The famine in Scotland in 1332 is attested in later sources but not in contemporary primary documents; the exact year is uncertain and may have occurred in 1331 or 1333. The Battle of Dupplin Moor is sometimes dated to August 1332, but some sources place it in 1333; the traditional date of 1332 is now standard. The Yuan Dynasty's peasant rebellions in 1332 are poorly documented in surviving sources; the year is assigned by convention based on later compilations.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1332 is sparse. Japanese events (Genkō Rebellion) are well-recorded. English and Scottish events are documented in later chronicles (Froissart, Scotichronicon) and English royal records. Chinese events rely on much later compilations (Yuan Shi, Ming Shilu) and are difficult to date precisely. The year falls in a period of significant geopolitical instability across multiple regions (Japan, Scotland, China), but surviving sources are unevenly distributed. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of Central Asia are absent from surviving written records.

graph_edges.note: The 1332 campaigns in the Genkō Rebellion led to the final victory in 1333
The famine and the Second War of Scottish Independence occurred simultaneously, exacerbating hardship
Yuan Dynasty instability and peasant rebellions led to the rise of figures like Zhu Yuanzhang
